questions.explanation: The notice's central purpose is to announce the Fall Career Fair and explain how students should prepare and register. A introduces past results not present; B introduces paid workshops absent from the text; C captures no stated location change.

items.text:
Course Evaluation Period: Fall Semester

The online course evaluation portal will open on Monday, December 2, and close at 11:59 PM on Friday, December 13. All students must complete an evaluation for each enrolled course through the campus portal at eval.university.edu.

Evaluations are submitted anonymously, and your instructor will not see any responses until final grades have been posted. Please set aside time during these two weeks to provide thoughtful feedback.

Keep the following in mind:

- Each evaluation takes about ten minutes to finish.
- Access to your final grades will be delayed until your evaluations are submitted.
- Students who complete all evaluations by December 6 will be entered in a drawing for a $50 campus bookstore gift card.
- Paper forms are available in Hartley Hall, Room 118, for students without reliable internet access.

The portal can be reached from any device, on or off campus. Responses are used to improve teaching and course design for future semesters.

Questions about the process should be directed to the Office of Academic Assessment at assessment@university.edu or (555) 204-7781.

questions.stem: According to the notice, what happens if a student does not submit their evaluations?

options.A: A late fee is added to the student's account.
options.B: A paper form is mailed to the student's address.
options.C: Access to final grades is delayed for that student.
options.D: Enrollment in future courses is temporarily blocked.

questions.explanation: The bulleted list states that 'Access to your final grades will be delayed until your evaluations are submitted,' so not submitting delays grade access. Option A borrows the fee idea but no late fee is mentioned; B reuses 'paper form' in a fabricated mailing context; D introduces enrollment blocking, which is absent from the notice.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about students who submit their evaluations by December 6?

options.A: They receive their final grades earlier than other students.
options.B: They gain a chance to win a bookstore gift card.
options.C: They are exempt from evaluating their remaining courses.
options.D: They are charged a reduced fee for the paper forms.

questions.explanation: The notice says students who complete all evaluations by December 6 'will be entered in a drawing for a $50 campus bookstore gift card,' implying a chance to win rather than a guaranteed prize. A reverses the grade logic (early submission does not speed grades, it only avoids delay); C wrongly implies exemption; D invents a fee for paper forms that are described as freely available.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this notice?

options.A: To announce that final grades have been posted online.
options.B: To explain how paper evaluation forms are distributed.
options.C: To advertise a gift card drawing held by the bookstore.
options.D: To inform students about the course evaluation process and deadlines.

questions.explanation: The notice as a whole announces the evaluation period, the portal, the deadlines, and the rules, so its purpose is to inform students about the evaluation process. A misstates the grade timeline; B and C each capture only one minor detail (paper forms, the gift card) and are too narrow to be the main purpose.

items.text:
Spring Community Garden Plot Registration

Registration for the campus Community Garden opens on Monday, March 3, and continues through Friday, March 21. Plots are located behind the Greenhouse on West Campus and are assigned on a first-come, first-served basis.

Each registered student or staff member may reserve one raised bed for the growing season, which runs from April through October. A refundable deposit of $20 is required at sign-up and will be returned in November once the plot has been cleared.

Gardeners are expected to observe the following guidelines:

- Plots must be watered and weeded regularly throughout the season.
- Only organic fertilizers and pest control may be used.
- Shared tools are stored in the shed and must be returned after each use.
- Any plot left untended for more than three weeks may be reassigned.

A brief orientation will be held on Saturday, March 22, at 10:00 AM near the Greenhouse. New gardeners are strongly encouraged to attend.

To register or ask questions, please contact the Grounds Office at garden@university.edu.

questions.stem: According to the notice, what is required when a gardener signs up for a plot?

options.A: A signed agreement from a faculty sponsor.
options.B: A set of personal gardening tools.
options.C: A refundable deposit of twenty dollars.
options.D: A completed orientation session beforehand.

questions.explanation: The notice states that 'A refundable deposit of $20 is required at sign-up,' which matches option C. A introduces a faculty sponsor not mentioned anywhere; B reverses the tool detail (tools are shared and stored in the shed, not supplied by the gardener); D swaps the timing, since orientation is held after registration, not before.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about a plot that is left untended for a month?

options.A: Its deposit is increased for the following season.
options.B: It may be given to a different gardener.
options.C: Its produce is donated to the Grounds Office.
options.D: It must be watered twice as often afterward.

questions.explanation: The guidelines say any plot left untended for more than three weeks 'may be reassigned,' and a month exceeds three weeks, so the plot may go to another gardener. A invents a deposit increase; C fabricates a produce donation using the 'Grounds Office' name in the wrong context; D introduces a watering penalty that is not stated.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this notice?

options.A: To announce garden plot registration and its rules.
options.B: To describe the layout of the West Campus greenhouse.
options.C: To advertise an orientation for experienced gardeners.
options.D: To explain how organic fertilizers should be applied.

questions.explanation: The notice opens by announcing registration dates and then lists deposit and usage rules, so its purpose is to announce registration and its guidelines. B focuses on a single location detail; C misstates the orientation as being for experienced gardeners when it targets new ones; D captures only one bulleted rule and is too narrow.

items.text:
Subject: Updated Printing Policy for the Library Computer Lab

Dear Students,

Starting Monday, February 10, the Hadley Library will introduce a new printing policy for all lab users. We've made these changes to reduce paper waste and keep printing affordable for everyone.

Each student now receives a free printing balance of 200 black-and-white pages per semester. After that allowance is used, black-and-white pages will be charged at $0.07 each, and color pages at $0.25 each. Your balance can be checked at any time through the student portal.

Please note the following:

- Print jobs must be released at the station within sixty minutes, or they will be deleted.
- Refunds for misprints can be requested at the front desk on the second floor.
- Large jobs over fifty pages should be sent to the Media Center in Room 230 instead.

Unused free pages do not carry over to the next semester. If you have questions about your account, stop by the help desk or email us at libtech@university.edu.

Best regards,
Hadley Library Technology Services

format_metadata.subject: Updated Printing Policy for the Library Computer Lab

questions.stem: According to the email, where should a student send a print job of more than fifty pages?

options.A: To the front desk on the second floor.
options.B: To the student portal printing queue.
options.C: To the Media Center in Room 230.
options.D: To the help desk in Hadley Library.

questions.explanation: The email instructs that 'Large jobs over fifty pages should be sent to the Media Center in Room 230 instead,' matching option C. A reuses the second-floor front desk, which handles refunds; B borrows 'student portal,' which is for checking balances; D names the help desk, which handles account questions, not large print jobs.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about a student's unused free pages at the end of the semester?

options.A: They can be sold back for a partial refund.
options.B: They are lost when the new semester begins.
options.C: They are converted into color printing credit.
options.D: They are doubled as a reward for low usage.

questions.explanation: The email states that 'Unused free pages do not carry over to the next semester,' so any remaining balance is lost when the new semester starts. A invents a buy-back refund; C fabricates a conversion to color credit; D introduces a doubling reward that the email never mentions.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this email?

options.A: To inform students about the library's new printing policy.
options.B: To request refunds for students affected by misprints.
options.C: To announce the opening of a new Media Center.
options.D: To remind students to check the second-floor desk.

questions.explanation: The email announces the new printing allowance, charges, and related rules, so its purpose is to inform students about the new printing policy. B describes only the refund detail; C wrongly frames the existing Media Center as newly opening; D elevates a minor logistical detail to the main point, making both too narrow.

items.text:
Annual Spring Sustainability Fair

The Office of Campus Life invites all students, faculty, and staff to the seventh Annual Spring Sustainability Fair, to be held on Thursday, April 17, from 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM on the Anderson Quad. The event will take place rain or shine; a covered tent will be set up near Anderson Hall in case of poor weather.

More than forty student organizations and local vendors will host booths featuring renewable energy demonstrations, a clothing swap, and workshops on composting. A reusable tote bag will be given to the first 150 visitors who check in at the welcome table.

Visitors should be aware of the following:

- Admission is free, though a $3.00 donation is suggested to support campus recycling programs.
- Food trucks will accept card payments only.
- Free bicycle valet parking will be available along College Avenue.

Volunteers are still needed for the morning setup shift and will receive a fair T-shirt and lunch. To volunteer or learn more, visit campuslife.university.edu or call the Office of Campus Life at (555) 318-2240.

questions.stem: According to the notice, what will the first 150 visitors to check in receive?

options.A: A free lunch from the food trucks.
options.B: A voucher for bicycle valet parking.
options.C: A reusable tote bag at the welcome table.
options.D: A fair T-shirt for attending the event.

questions.explanation: The notice says 'A reusable tote bag will be given to the first 150 visitors who check in at the welcome table,' matching option C. A borrows the food trucks but the lunch is for volunteers; B reuses bicycle valet parking, which is free for everyone, not a reward for early check-in; D takes the T-shirt, which is given to volunteers rather than the first visitors.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about visitors who want to buy food at the fair?

options.A: They must reserve a meal in advance online.
options.B: They will need to bring a payment card.
options.C: They are required to make a $3.00 donation.
options.D: They can only eat inside the covered tent.

questions.explanation: Because the notice states 'Food trucks will accept card payments only,' a visitor wanting food would need a payment card rather than cash. A invents an advance reservation; C confuses the optional fair donation with a food requirement; D misapplies the bad-weather tent as a dining restriction, neither of which the notice supports.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this notice?

options.A: To recruit volunteers for the recycling program.
options.B: To explain the campus policy on food truck payments.
options.C: To announce a sustainability fair and its details.
options.D: To request donations for new bicycle parking.

questions.explanation: The notice announces the date, time, location, activities, and logistics of the Sustainability Fair, so its main purpose is to announce the fair and its details. A captures only the volunteer note; B and D each focus on a single bulleted item (payment method, donation) and are too narrow to represent the whole notice.

items.text:
The Henderson Library will undergo scheduled maintenance on Saturday, March 14, from 8:00 AM to 2:00 PM. During this period, the second and third floors will be closed for electrical upgrades. The main entrance and ground-floor study commons will remain open throughout.

Students are reminded of the following:

- All laptops and personal belongings must be removed from the upper floors by Friday, March 13, at 5:00 PM.
- Items left behind will be relocated to the Lost and Found desk in Room 110.
- Printing services on the third floor will be temporarily unavailable; please use the ground-floor printers near the Help Desk.
- A $5.00 retrieval fee applies to any belongings collected after March 20.

Reserved group study rooms scheduled during the maintenance window have been automatically cancelled. Affected students will receive a confirmation email and may rebook through the library portal once service resumes. For questions, please contact the Circulation Office at circ@henderson.edu or visit Room 110 during regular hours.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this notice?

options.A: To explain how students can reserve group study rooms online
options.B: To inform students about a temporary library closure for maintenance
options.C: To announce the permanent relocation of printing services
options.D: To describe new electrical safety rules for the entire campus

questions.explanation: The notice synthesizes information across the text: it announces scheduled maintenance, the closure of the upper floors, and the temporary unavailability of services. The overall purpose is to inform students about the temporary closure, not to explain reservations (A, a detail), announce a permanent change (C, contradicted by 'temporarily unavailable'), or describe campus-wide rules (D, too broad).

questions.stem: According to the notice, what must students do by Friday, March 13?

options.A: Rebook their group study rooms through the portal
options.B: Contact the Circulation Office about printing services
options.C: Remove their belongings from the upper floors
options.D: Collect any lost items from the Help Desk

questions.explanation: The notice states that 'all laptops and personal belongings must be removed from the upper floors by Friday, March 13, at 5:00 PM.' Option A reverses the timeline (rebooking happens after service resumes). Option B borrows 'Circulation Office' and 'printing' but combines them wrongly. Option D swaps the Lost and Found in Room 110 with the Help Desk.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about a student who retrieves a left-behind laptop on March 22?

options.A: They will rebook the room through the library portal
options.B: They will be charged a retrieval fee
options.C: They will receive a confirmation email
options.D: They will use the ground-floor printers

questions.explanation: The notice states a '$5.00 retrieval fee applies to any belongings collected after March 20.' Since March 22 is after that date, it can be inferred the student will be charged. Option A applies to cancelled study rooms, not belongings. Option C refers to students with cancelled reservations. Option D refers to printing, an unrelated service.

items.text:
Subject: Updated Parking Policy for Fall Semester

Dear Residents of Wexford Hall,

I'm writing to let you know about important changes to the campus parking policy that take effect on September 1. These updates are designed to improve safety and ensure fair access to the limited spaces in Lot C.

Starting this fall, all resident vehicles must display a valid Lot C permit, which costs $45 per semester. Permits can be purchased online through the student portal and must be renewed each term. Vehicles parked in Lot C without a visible permit after September 8 will receive a $25 citation.

Please note the following:

- Overnight parking is permitted only for registered residents.
- Visitors must obtain a temporary pass from the front desk in Wexford Hall.
- The lot will be closed for resurfacing from September 20 to September 22.

If you have already registered your vehicle, you don't need to take any further action. For other questions, please reach out to me directly at parking@wexford.edu.

Best regards,
Daniel Reyes
Residence Parking Coordinator

format_metadata.subject: Updated Parking Policy for Fall Semester

questions.stem: According to the email, what must residents do to park in Lot C this fall?

options.A: Display a valid permit costing $45 per semester
options.B: Register their vehicle at the front desk each week
options.C: Obtain a temporary pass from the residence coordinator
options.D: Renew their citation through the online student portal

questions.explanation: The email states that 'all resident vehicles must display a valid Lot C permit, which costs $45 per semester.' Option B borrows 'front desk' and 'register' but the front desk is for visitor passes. Option C swaps the permit with the visitor temporary pass. Option D wrongly attaches 'renew' and 'portal' to a citation rather than a permit.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about a resident who parks in Lot C without a permit on September 10?

options.A: They will need to purchase a temporary visitor pass
options.B: They will have their vehicle registered automatically
options.C: They will be required to pay a citation
options.D: They will be allowed overnight parking access

questions.explanation: The email states that vehicles without a visible permit 'after September 8 will receive a $25 citation.' Since September 10 is after that date, the resident will be required to pay. Option A applies to visitors, not residents. Option B reverses the policy, which requires action from the resident. Option D contradicts the rule, as overnight parking requires registration.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this email?

options.A: To remind residents to renew their visitor passes online
options.B: To announce changes to the campus parking policy
options.C: To explain why Lot C will be permanently closed
options.D: To request that residents register at the front desk

questions.explanation: The email synthesizes several updates—permits, citations, and closures—under the stated goal of informing residents about 'important changes to the campus parking policy.' Option A captures only a minor detail and misattributes renewal to visitor passes. Option C contradicts the temporary resurfacing dates. Option D is too narrow and applies to visitors.

questions.stem: According to the email, when will Lot C be closed for resurfacing?

options.A: From September 1 through the end of the term
options.B: From September 8 until permits are renewed
options.C: From September 20 to September 22
options.D: From September 22 until the front desk reopens

questions.explanation: The email states that 'the lot will be closed for resurfacing from September 20 to September 22.' Option A swaps in the policy start date (September 1). Option B uses the citation date (September 8) in the wrong context. Option D reverses the end date and attaches an unrelated front desk condition.

items.text:
NOTICE: Annual Locker Clean-Out and Renewal

The Recreation Office will conduct the annual locker clean-out from Wednesday, May 22 through Friday, May 24, in Anderson Hall, Room 118. All students who wish to keep their assigned locker for the fall semester must complete the renewal process by 5:00 PM on Friday, May 24.

Renewal procedures:
- A renewal fee of $15 must be paid online through the campus portal before arriving in person.
- Students must bring their student ID and current locker key when picking up the new combination tag.
- Lockers not renewed by the deadline will be emptied by maintenance staff. Personal items will be placed in the lost-and-found office and held for thirty days.

Locker access will be suspended on Saturday, May 25, while cleaning is performed. Regular access resumes Monday, May 27.

For questions, please contact Ms. Hollis at the Recreation Office, Anderson Hall, Room 102, between 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM on weekdays.

options.B: Pay the renewal fee online through the campus portal
options.C: Email a copy of their current student schedule
options.D: Bring a printed copy of the renewal confirmation

questions.explanation: The notice specifies the $15 renewal fee must be paid online through the campus portal before arrival.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about students who miss the renewal deadline?

options.A: Their lockers will be reassigned to another student immediately
options.B: Their belongings will be discarded by maintenance staff
options.C: Their personal items will be transferred to lost-and-found
options.D: Their access will be restored after a thirty-day delay

questions.explanation: The notice states personal items removed from non-renewed lockers will be placed in the lost-and-found office for thirty days.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of the notice?

options.A: To announce a fee increase for campus locker rentals
options.B: To describe seasonal maintenance work in Anderson Hall
options.C: To explain how students should renew their lockers before the deadline
options.D: To remind students about lost-and-found procedures on campus

questions.explanation: The notice's central focus is the renewal procedure students must follow to keep their lockers for the next semester.

items.text:
Subject: Dormitory Check-Out Procedures — Action Required

Hi everyone,

As the spring semester ends, please review the check-out steps for Whitman Hall. All residents must vacate their rooms by 12:00 PM on Saturday, May 18.

Before departure, please complete the following:
1. Schedule a room inspection with your floor RA between Tuesday, May 14, and Friday, May 17. Inspections take roughly twenty minutes.
2. Remove all personal belongings. Furniture must be returned to its original arrangement, and the trash must be taken to the loading dock behind the building.
3. Return both your room key and mailbox key to the front desk. Replacement keys carry a $25 fee, so please don't lose them.

If you have not yet booked your inspection, send a message through the housing portal today. Late check-outs will be billed an additional $50 fee, charged to your student account.

Thanks for a great semester, and best of luck with finals!

Best regards,
Noah Bennett
Resident Advisor, Whitman Hall

MAPLE GROVE STUDENT CAFÉ — STUDY-HOUR SPECIALS

Served daily from 2:00 PM to 5:00 PM in the Student Union, Lower Level.

* Bottomless Drip Coffee: $2.00 (refills $0.50, free for honor-roll students with valid ID)
* House Bagel with Cream Cheese: $3.50 — add avocado for $1.25
* Toasted Veggie Wrap: $5.75 — gluten-free wrap available for $0.75 extra
* Mediterranean Bowl: $7.25 — rotates daily, includes hummus and seasonal grain
* Fresh-Squeezed Lemonade: $2.50
* Energy Cookie (oats, dark chocolate, walnut): $1.75

STUDY-HOUR DEAL: Any drink + any baked item for a flat $4.00 between 2 and 5 PM only. Cannot combine with other discounts.

PAYMENT: Meal swipes accepted Monday–Thursday. Dining dollars and credit cards accepted any time. Cash payments incur a $0.25 processing surcharge after 7 PM.

Group study tables require a minimum order of $15.00 per table. Reservations through the dining app are encouraged.

items.text:
Subject: Coordinating Friday's Acid-Base Titration Lab

Hi Priya,

I wanted to confirm a few things before our chemistry lab on Friday afternoon, March 14. Dr. Holloway said our titration of the unknown sample is due by the end of class, so we'll need to be efficient.

A few logistics: I've reserved Station 7 in Room 304 from 2:00 to 4:30 PM through the lab portal. I'll bring the printed procedure and the calibration check sheet. Could you grab the standardized sodium hydroxide solution from the prep room before class? Ms. Tran said students need to sign it out at the desk, and last time the supply ran low after 1:30.

For the analysis, I'll handle the burette readings and you can record the indicator color changes. We should both photograph the final titration curve for the report. Quick reminder that the lab notebook entry is graded — Dr. Holloway mentioned at the last session that incomplete entries cost the team three points.

If anything changes, text me by Thursday evening.

Thanks!

Marcus

items.text:
Subject: Campus Radio Station — Volunteer Recruitment

Hi Briar,

I'm writing to invite you to volunteer at WCRX, the student-run campus radio station, for the spring semester. We are recruiting fifteen new volunteers across three teams: on-air hosts, technical operators, and weekend producers. Each volunteer commits to a minimum of three hours per week starting March 1, and all training takes place during the last week of February in Lockwood Hall, Studio B.

A $25 station fee covers your access card, headphone rental, and the volunteer T-shirt. Returning volunteers who served last semester pay only $10. If you're interested in joining the team, please complete the application form on our website by February 18 and send a brief audio sample to Mr. Patel at the station office. Selected volunteers will be notified by February 22 at the latest.

Let me know if you have any questions before the deadline.

Best,
Lena Marquez
Station Manager, WCRX

Subject: Flu Shot Appointment Confirmation

Dear Theo,

This email confirms your flu shot appointment at the Westgate Health Clinic on Tuesday, October 17 at 11:15 AM. Please arrive at the second-floor reception desk in Westgate Hall ten minutes before your scheduled time and bring your student ID and insurance card.

The nurse on duty will be Ms. Yang. After the injection, you will be asked to remain in the waiting area for fifteen minutes so that staff can monitor for any unusual reactions. The standard charge is $0 for students with the campus health plan; visitors and dependents pay a $25 fee on site.

If you need to reschedule, please respond to this email at least twenty-four hours in advance so the slot can be reassigned. Walk-in appointments are not available during the busy first week of November.

Best regards,
Westgate Health Clinic

items.text:
Dining Hall Allergen Policy Update

The University Dining Services will implement a new allergen labeling policy beginning Monday, September 11. The policy affects all four main dining halls and applies to every meal period from 7:00 AM to 9:00 PM daily.

Key changes:
- All hot-line dishes will be labeled with the top nine allergens (milk, eggs, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soy, and sesame).
- A dedicated allergen-free preparation station will operate in Sutter Hall.
- Students with a confirmed dietary restriction may receive a $5 weekly meal credit by signing up at the Health Services office.

To enroll, please contact Ms. Quan in the Health Services office, Room 215 of the Wellness Center, between 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM weekdays. Pre-registration ensures that ingredient cards are printed before each meal period. Students are reminded to check signage at every station before serving themselves.

For full guidelines, visit the dining portal.

questions.stem: According to the notice, what must students do to receive the meal credit?

options.A: Speak with the dining manager during meals
options.B: Sign up at the Health Services office
options.C: Email Ms. Quan a list of allergens
options.D: Visit the allergen-free station each day

DINING HALL ALLERGEN POLICY UPDATE

Effective Monday, September 8, the Anderson Dining Hall will adopt a revised allergen policy designed to better protect students with food sensitivities. All hot-station menus will be labeled with the top nine allergens, and a dedicated allergen-free station will be opened at lunch.

Key changes include:

- Allergen ingredient cards will be displayed at each station from 7:00 AM to 9:00 PM
- A separate allergen-free prep counter will operate at the south entrance
- Students with documented allergies can request meals prepared by the head chef with 24 hours notice
- The new allergen-free meal plan add-on costs $45 per semester and can be activated through the dining portal

Students are encouraged to register their dietary needs with Ms. Garner at the Dining Services Office, Room 110, before September 5. Late registration is accepted but cannot be guaranteed for the first week.

Questions should be directed to dining@university.edu.

items.text:
LAUNDRY ROOM RENOVATION NOTICE

The basement laundry rooms in Hartley Residence Hall will be temporarily closed for renovation from Monday, October 14 through Friday, November 1. During this period, all washers and dryers will be replaced with newer high-efficiency units, and electrical wiring will be upgraded.

Students should be aware of the following arrangements:

- The Bryant Hall laundry room, located two blocks east, will remain open 24 hours for Hartley residents during the closure
- A free shuttle will run between Hartley and Bryant every twenty minutes from 6:00 PM to 11:00 PM on weekdays
- Any laundry left in the Hartley machines on October 13 will be relocated to the lost-and-found bin in the lobby for one week before being discarded
- Residents needing emergency laundry service can contact Mr. Sutton at the Housing Office

The new machines will offer mobile-pay access through the housing app, eliminating the need for coin purchases. The renovation is being completed by Carter Facilities, the contractor selected by Residential Services.

options.D: It will be discarded immediately by Carter Facilities

Subject: Pottery Studio Reservation – Confirmation for May 4

Hi Jenna,

I'm confirming your reservation for the ceramics studio in Carver Hall, Room 12, on Monday, May 4, from 1:00 PM to 5:00 PM. This will give you a full afternoon to finish glazing your final project before the May 9 critique.

A few reminders:
- Please bring your own apron and at least three bisque-fired pieces ready for glazing.
- The $25 kiln-firing fee must be paid in cash to the studio assistant before you begin.
- Studio rules require closed-toe shoes; you won't be admitted in sandals.

If you need to cancel, please email me at least 48 hours in advance so another student can use the slot. Looking forward to seeing your finished work!

Best,
Marcus Lee
Ceramics Lab Coordinator

RECREATION CENTER POLICY UPDATE
Effective Date: Monday, September 9
Location: Harrison Recreation Center

The Harrison Recreation Center will implement the following updated facility policies beginning Monday, September 9. These changes are being introduced to improve member safety and equipment availability during peak hours.

Updated Hours and Access:
• Weekday hours extended to 5:30 AM through 11:30 PM
• Saturday hours reduced to 8:00 AM through 8:00 PM
• Sunday hours unchanged at 10:00 AM through 6:00 PM

New Policies:
• Cardio equipment is now subject to a 30-minute time limit when others are waiting
• A $10 day-pass option is available for non-member campus visitors
• Lockers must be cleared daily; padlocks left overnight will be cut by staff
• Reservations for racquetball courts open at 6:00 AM each morning

Members who wish to review the full policy document may pick up a printed copy at the Welcome Desk or download it from the Recreation Center website. For questions, please contact Member Services at 555-7745 weekdays between 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM.

Notice: Launch of CampusConnect App

Date: April 15, 9:00 AM
Location: Student Union, Room 210

The Office of Student Affairs is pleased to announce the launch of CampusConnect, a new mobile application designed to help students access services, view schedules, and receive real-time alerts about campus events. A demonstration session will be held in Room 210 on April 15 from 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM, hosted by Ms. Patel from the Information Technology Office.

Students who attend the launch session will receive a complimentary launch month at no charge. Please bring a charged smartphone and your student ID. For questions, contact campusconnect@university.edu.

Notice: E-Waste Recycling Drive

Date: May 22-24, 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM
Location: Parking Lot D, Engineering Quad

The Office of Sustainability will host a three-day electronic waste recycling drive to help students, faculty, and staff dispose of unwanted electronics safely. All collected items will be sorted by certified handlers and recycled in accordance with state regulations.

Accepted items:

- Laptops and tablets
- Mobile phones and chargers
- Printers and small appliances
- Cables, batteries, and computer mice

Not accepted:

- Large household appliances such as refrigerators
- Televisions over forty inches
- Items containing liquid or hazardous chemicals

Participants must remove personal data before drop-off; the office does not provide data wiping services. A free reusable tote will be given to the first 100 participants each day. Volunteers from the Engineering Sustainability Club will assist with sorting. Questions may be directed to ewaste@university.edu.

Best,
Renata Diaz

items.text:
Introduction to Biology — Lab Component Guidelines (Spring Semester)

The laboratory portion of BIOL 101 contributes 35% of the final course grade and is required for all enrolled students. Weekly lab reports account for 20%, two practical exams contribute another 10%, and lab attendance and preparation make up the remaining 5%.

All lab reports must be submitted through the online portal by 11:59 PM on the Sunday following each scheduled lab. Late reports are accepted within 48 hours of the deadline for a 15% penalty. Reports submitted after 48 hours will not receive credit.

A non-refundable safety equipment fee of $25 must be paid by January 22 at the Bursar's Office in Anderson Hall. The fee covers safety goggles, lab notebooks, and access to the microscope library. Students who miss more than three lab sessions without an excused absence will be required to repeat the course.

Please contact your TA, Ms. Lin, in Room 204 during her Tuesday office hours (2:00-4:00 PM) for any questions about grading or makeup labs.

questions.explanation: The syllabus states that attendance and preparation make up the remaining 5%.

questions.explanation: The syllabus says the $25 safety fee must be paid by January 22 at the Bursar's Office.

options.B: The student will not receive any credit for that report.
options.C: The student will lose only 15% of the report's value.
options.D: The student will need to repeat the entire course.

questions.explanation: Reports submitted more than 48 hours after the deadline will not receive credit.

Wi-Fi Security Certificate Update — March 18

The campus wireless network certificate will be updated on March 18 between 6:00 AM and 8:00 AM. After the update, all devices currently connected to CampusNet must reconfigure their secure connection before regaining access.

Details of the update:

- Affected service: CampusNet secure wireless
- Outage window: 6:00 AM - 8:00 AM, March 18
- Devices impacted: laptops, smartphones, tablets, and printers
- Drop-in support: Library Help Desk, 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, March 18-19
- Self-service guide: available on the IT portal

A $10 device-setup support fee will be charged for technicians making in-room visits to faculty offices, though walk-in help at the Library Help Desk remains free for all students. Devices that fail to update after March 20 may be temporarily blocked from network access.

Questions may be directed to the Network Operations Center at netops@example.edu or to Mr. Kowalski at extension 4582. Students are encouraged to update before the weekend to avoid service interruptions during midterm week.

DORMITORY PARKING POLICY UPDATE — Effective April 15

The Office of Residence Life is issuing the following changes to the resident parking program at Beckett Hall and Carlton Hall:

- All overnight parking permits will move from monthly billing to semester billing. The new charge is $85 per semester, due at the start of each term. Payments may be made online through the housing portal or in person at the Residence Life office in Carlton Hall, Room 110.
- Visitor passes will be available for purchase at the front desk between 7:00 AM and 9:00 PM at a rate of $3 per night. A maximum of three visitor nights per resident is permitted each month.
- Residents must register their vehicles in the housing portal by April 30. Unregistered vehicles will be ticketed beginning May 1, and any ticket exceeding $50 will require an appeal hearing.

Questions regarding billing or vehicle registration should be directed to the Residence Life office at extension 4422.

Hi Jordan,

I hope you had a good weekend. I'm writing to ask whether you could cover my Thursday evening shift at the Library Café this week. My research seminar got rescheduled to that same time slot, and I won't be able to make it in.

If you can take the shift from 5:00 to 9:00 PM on Thursday, March 21, I'm happy to cover any shift of yours during finals week — including the busy Saturday 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM block, which pays the $2 weekend bonus. I'll also bring you coffee on me as thanks.

Please let me know by Wednesday afternoon so I can update Ms. Tanaka if neither of us is available. Apologies for the late notice, and thanks for considering it.

Best,
Riley

Subject: Overdue notice for 'Patterns of Ancient Trade'

Dear Ms. Caldwell,

This is a reminder from the University Library that the book 'Patterns of Ancient Trade' borrowed from the Anthropology section is now nine days overdue. The original due date was Tuesday, March 5.

To avoid additional fees, please return the book to the circulation desk on the second floor of Henderson Library by Saturday, March 16. Current charges stand at $9.00, and they will continue to accrue at $1.00 per day until the book is returned or paid for.

If you have already returned the book or believe this notice was sent in error, please reply to this message or visit the circulation desk between 8:00 AM and 6:00 PM, Monday through Friday. Our staff can review your account and clear any mistake.

Thank you for your prompt attention.

Best regards,
The University Library Team

items.text:
Notice: Updated Allergen Policy for Campus Dining Halls

Effective September 1, all on-campus dining halls will implement a revised allergen management policy to enhance student safety. This policy applies to Anderson Hall, Smith Commons, and the Student Center Cafeteria during regular operating hours (7:30 AM to 8:00 PM).

- All menu items will display clear allergen labels for the top nine allergens, including peanuts, tree nuts, dairy, eggs, soy, wheat, fish, shellfish, and sesame.
- Students with severe allergies must register with the Dining Services office by August 25 to receive a personalized meal plan. A $10 processing fee applies.
- Cross-contact prevention measures include separate preparation areas and color-coded utensils.
- Any student who experiences an allergic reaction should report it immediately to the nearest dining hall manager.

For questions, contact Dining Services at (555) 123-4567 or visit Room 204 in the Student Center.

Campus Printing Quota and Kiosk Changes

Effective May 15, the university will implement a new printing quota system. All students will receive a base quota of 200 pages per semester. Additional pages can be purchased at $0.05 per page via the online portal. The printing kiosks in Anderson Hall, Room 204, will be updated on May 10 from 9:00 AM to 4:00 PM. During this time, these kiosks will be unavailable. A $5.00 penalty will be applied for each failed print job that is not cleared within 24 hours. Please follow these guidelines:

- Confirm your print job before sending.
- Collect all prints promptly.
- Report any kiosk issues to the IT Service Desk in Smith Library, Room 101.

For questions, contact printing@campus.edu.

Wi-Fi Security Certificate Update Notice

To enhance network safety, the Division of Information Technology will update the campus Wi-Fi security certificate on September 12. This update will affect all devices connected to the "CampusNet" network. Users must install the new certificate by September 15 to maintain access. The update is automatic for university-owned devices. Personal devices, however, require manual installation. Follow these steps:

- Connect to CampusNet.
- Visit the portal at it.campus.edu/cert.
- Download and install the certificate.
- Restart your device.

Failure to install the certificate will result in temporary Wi-Fi access loss. A $10.00 late fee will be charged for assistance requests after September 20. For help, contact the IT Help Desk in Room 105, Baker Hall, or call 555-0100.

Subject: Join the Campus Radio Station – Volunteer Recruitment

Hi everyone,

Are you passionate about music, news, or storytelling? The Campus Radio Station (WUCR) is recruiting volunteers for the Fall semester. We're looking for hosts, audio engineers, and content creators. No prior experience is needed—we'll train you during orientation on September 12 from 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM in Room 204 of the Student Union.

Volunteers must be available for at least one 2-hour shift per week. You'll get hands-on experience with broadcasting equipment and learn how to produce your own show. Plus, volunteers receive a free parking pass for the semester and a $10 gift card to the campus coffee shop after completing 10 shifts.

To apply, fill out the online form by September 5. Spaces are limited, so don't wait! Contact us at wucr@campus.edu if you have questions.

Best regards,
Maya Johnson
WUCR Volunteer Coordinator

Subject: Chemistry Lab Coordination – Experiment #3

Hi Jordan,

I'm writing to coordinate our next chemistry lab experiment (Experiment #3: Acid-Base Titration) scheduled for this Friday, November 10, at 2:00 PM in Lab 302 of the Science Building. We'll need to bring our lab coats, safety goggles, and the pre-lab worksheet from our last session. I'll also bring the $5 fee for the titration kit that we need to purchase from the stockroom.

Could you please check if we have enough distilled water and indicator solution? I think we're low on both. Also, we must submit the lab report by November 17 at 5:00 PM to avoid a $10 late penalty. Let's plan to meet on November 14 at 3:00 PM in the library to work on it together.

Let me know if that works for you!

Thanks,
Priya

DevStudent01

Hey everyone! I just launched a new app called StudyBuddy that I built over winter break. It helps you find study partners for your classes based on your schedule and subject preferences. You can filter by course, campus location (like the Library or Student Union), and even by study style (quiet vs. group).

Best part – it's completely free to use! No ads, no in-app purchases. I spent about $50 on server costs but that's it. I'm just trying to make studying a little easier for all of us.

I'd love for you to try it out and give me feedback. What features would you add? Drop your thoughts in the comments or DM me. Also, if you find any bugs, let me know! I'll be updating it every weekend.

#StudyBuddy #AppLaunch #StudentLife #FreeApp

Subject: Urban Sketching Walk – This Saturday!

Hi everyone,

Are you looking for a creative way to explore campus? The Art Club is hosting an Urban Sketching Walk this Saturday, May 15, from 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM. We'll meet at the fountain in front of Anderson Hall.

No experience is necessary, and all materials are provided for a $5.00 fee. Just bring your enthusiasm and comfortable walking shoes! We'll sketch several campus landmarks, including the old library and the sculpture garden. Our guide, Ms. Elena Rossi, is a professional illustrator who'll share tips on perspective and shading.

Space is limited to 25 participants, so please register by Friday, May 14, at 5:00 PM on the Art Club's portal. You can pay the fee online or bring cash to the event. If the weather is bad, we'll move the walk indoors to the Student Union.

Best regards,

Jamie Park
Art Club President

Subject: Film Screening: The Art of Animation

Hi Film Club Members,

We're excited to announce our next event: a screening of "The Art of Animation" this Friday, March 10, at 7:30 PM in the Johnson Auditorium. This documentary explores the history of animation from classic hand-drawn cells to modern CGI.

Tickets are $2.00 for Film Club members and $5.00 for non-members. You can purchase them at the door or in advance at the Student Union information desk. Seating is first-come, first-served, and the auditorium holds 120 people. We recommend arriving early to get a good spot!

After the screening, there will be a Q&A session with Professor Kim from the Film Studies Department, who consulted on the documentary. This is a great chance to ask questions and learn more about the animation industry.

Hope to see you there!

Liam O'Brien
Film Club Treasurer

items.text:
Annual E-Waste Recycling Drive — Save the Date

The Office of Campus Sustainability will host its annual e-waste recycling drive on Saturday, October 18, from 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM in the Henderson Hall parking lot. All students, faculty, and staff are encouraged to dispose of outdated electronics responsibly rather than discarding them in regular trash bins.

Accepted items include laptops, monitors, printers, cables, and small kitchen appliances. Please note the following guidelines:

- All personal data must be erased before drop-off; the University is not responsible for information left on devices.
- A nominal handling fee of $5.00 will be charged for each television or large monitor.
- Batteries should be removed and placed in the separate collection bin provided on-site.

Volunteers will be stationed throughout the lot to assist participants and direct traffic. Proceeds collected will be donated to the campus tree-planting fund. For questions regarding accepted materials or to arrange a bulk pickup, contact the Sustainability Office at sustainability@university.edu or call 555-0192.

questions.stem: According to the notice, what is required of participants before they drop off a device?

options.A: Erasing all personal data from the device
options.B: Removing the cables from the device
options.C: Registering the device at the Sustainability Office
options.D: Paying a fee for every accepted item

questions.explanation: The notice states that 'All personal data must be erased before drop-off,' which matches option A. Option B confuses cables (an accepted item) with a removal requirement; only batteries must be removed. Option C invents a registration step not mentioned. Option D distorts the fee rule, which applies only to televisions and large monitors, not every item.

questions.stem: The word 'nominal' in the second bullet point is closest in meaning to

options.A: Mandatory
options.B: Small
options.C: Refundable
options.D: Occasional

questions.explanation: In context, a 'nominal handling fee of $5.00' refers to a small or token amount, so B is correct. A ('mandatory') describes whether it is required rather than its size. C ('refundable') and D ('occasional') introduce qualities the passage never assigns to the fee.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the money collected at the drive?

options.A: It will be returned to participants after the event.
options.B: It will be used to cover the cost of the volunteers.
options.C: It will support an environmental project on campus.
options.D: It will be added to the Sustainability Office budget.

questions.explanation: The notice says proceeds 'will be donated to the campus tree-planting fund,' a clearly environmental purpose, supporting C. A reverses the logic, since donated proceeds are not returned. B borrows the passage word 'volunteers' but wrongly links the money to paying them, when volunteers are simply stationed to assist. D names a plausible but unstated destination for the funds.

items.text:
Subject: Reminder — Summer Internship Applications Due Friday

Dear Daniel,

This is a friendly reminder from the Career Services Center that applications for the Summer Industry Internship Program are due this Friday, March 14, by 5:00 PM. We noticed that your profile in the careers portal is started but not yet submitted, and we don't want you to miss this opportunity.

Before you submit, please make sure your resume and a one-page cover letter are uploaded to the portal. You'll also need a confirmation of enrollment, which you can download directly from the student records page. Applications missing any of these three documents cannot be reviewed, so it's worth double-checking everything tonight.

If you'd like a staff member to look over your materials first, we hold drop-in advising in Room 210 of Carver Hall until 4:00 PM each weekday. No appointment is necessary, but spots fill quickly in the afternoons. Feel free to reply to this email if you have any questions.

Best regards,
Maria Lopez
Career Services Center

format_metadata.subject: Reminder — Summer Internship Applications Due Friday

options.A: A letter of recommendation from a professor

options.C: A completed advising appointment form
options.D: A summary of his previous work experience

questions.explanation: The email lists three required documents: a resume, a one-page cover letter, and 'a confirmation of enrollment.' Since the stem names the first two, the remaining item is B. A introduces a recommendation letter that is never mentioned. C borrows the passage word 'advising' but advising is optional, not a required form. D sounds plausible for an internship but is not among the stated documents.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of the email?

options.A: To announce a new summer internship program
options.B: To remind Daniel to finish submitting his application
options.C: To schedule an advising appointment for Daniel
options.D: To explain why Daniel's application was rejected

questions.explanation: The email opens by calling itself a reminder that applications are due Friday and notes Daniel's profile is 'started but not yet submitted,' making B the overall purpose. A is too narrow and wrong in focus, since the program already exists and is not being newly announced. C captures only the optional drop-in advising detail. D contradicts the email, which urges completion rather than reporting a rejection.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about visiting the drop-in advising in the afternoon?

options.B: It is available only to students who make an appointment.

options.D: It is held in a different building each weekday.

questions.explanation: The email notes that drop-in advising runs until 4:00 PM but 'spots fill quickly in the afternoons,' implying help may be hard to get later in the day, which supports C. A overstates the benefit, as advising reviews materials but does not guarantee approval. B reverses the stated policy that 'no appointment is necessary.' D contradicts the fixed location, Room 210 of Carver Hall.

items.text:
Notice: Temporary Closure of the West Wing Study Commons

Date: September 8
Location: Harmon Library, Third Floor

The West Wing Study Commons will be closed from Monday, September 15, through Friday, September 19, for the installation of new ventilation equipment. During this period, all 24-hour study access will be relocated to the East Reading Room on the second floor.

Students who reserved group rooms in the West Wing should note the following:

- Existing reservations will be automatically transferred to comparable rooms in the East Reading Room.
- A confirmation email will be sent to your campus address by September 12.
- Lockers in the West Wing must be emptied before 6:00 PM on September 12. Items left behind will be moved to the lost-and-found desk.
- A replacement key for any lost locker key may be obtained for a $5 fee at the circulation desk.

The West Wing is expected to reopen on Saturday, September 20, with extended weekend hours. Quiet study is still available throughout the building during construction, though some noise may occur between 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM.

For questions, please contact the library help desk at help@harmonlibrary.edu or visit the front office.

format_metadata.title: Notice: Temporary Closure of the West Wing Study Commons

options.A: Confirm their group room reservations by email
options.B: Remove their belongings from the West Wing lockers
options.C: Pay a $5 fee at the circulation desk
options.D: Relocate to the East Reading Room study area

questions.explanation: The notice states that lockers in the West Wing must be emptied before 6:00 PM on September 12. Option A swaps the September 12 date with the confirmation email (which is sent, not done by students). Option C uses the real $5 fee but applies it to a different, optional situation (a lost key). Option D borrows the East Reading Room but attaches it to the wrong deadline.

Option A reverses this logic with the absolute 'completely unavailable.' Option B contradicts the transfer of reservations to comparable rooms. Option C invents a fee for overnight access that the text never mentions.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this notice?

options.A: To inform students about a temporary closure and related arrangements
options.B: To announce permanent new weekend hours for the entire library
options.C: To advertise the installation of upgraded ventilation equipment
options.D: To remind students about the cost of replacing lost locker keys

questions.explanation: The notice's overall purpose is to inform students of the West Wing's temporary closure and the resulting changes to access, reservations, and lockers. Option B exaggerates with 'permanent' and 'entire library.' Option C captures only one detail (the equipment). Option D is too narrow, focusing on a single minor point about locker key fees.

items.text:
Subject: New Procedure for Reserving Lab Equipment

Dear Engineering Students,

Starting October 1, all reservations for equipment in the Fabrication Lab must be submitted through the online maintenance portal rather than the paper sign-up sheet. We're making this change so that equipment can be inspected and serviced between bookings, which should reduce the breakdowns many of you reported last semester.

To reserve a machine, log in to the portal with your student ID, select an available time slot, and confirm your booking at least 24 hours in advance. A refundable $10 deposit is required for the laser cutter and the 3D printers; the deposit is returned automatically once staff confirm the equipment was left clean and undamaged.

Please note that walk-in use will no longer be permitted after October 1. If you cannot make a reserved slot, cancel it online so another student can claim the time. Repeated no-shows may result in a one-week suspension of your booking privileges.

If you have trouble accessing the portal, stop by Room 112 in Whitmore Hall during staff hours, 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM.

Best regards,
Daniel Okafor
Lab Operations Coordinator

options.A: A signature on the paper sign-up sheet in the lab
options.B: A refundable deposit submitted along with the booking
options.C: A staff inspection completed before each time slot
options.D: A written request delivered to Room 112 in person

questions.explanation: The email states a refundable $10 deposit is required for the laser cutter and 3D printers. Option A refers to the old paper sheet that is being discontinued. Option C uses real words ('staff', 'inspection') but the inspection happens between bookings, not as a reservation requirement. Option D borrows Room 112 but applies it to a fabricated written-request process.

options.A: They will receive a full refund of their equipment deposit
options.B: They must switch back to using the paper sign-up sheet
options.C: They may temporarily lose the ability to book equipment
options.D: They will be charged an additional fee for each absence

questions.explanation: The email warns that repeated no-shows may result in a one-week suspension of booking privileges, implying a temporary loss of access. Option A reverses the consequence into a reward. Option B introduces the discontinued paper sheet as a penalty, which is not supported. Option D invents an extra fee that the text never mentions.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this email?

options.A: To remind students of the staff hours in Whitmore Hall
options.B: To explain why equipment broke down during last semester
options.C: To announce a new online system for reserving lab equipment
options.D: To request that students clean machines after every booking

questions.explanation: The email's overall purpose is to announce the shift to an online portal for reserving lab equipment and explain how it works. Option A captures only a minor closing detail. Option B addresses one reason for the change, not the main point. Option D focuses on a single condition tied to the deposit rather than the email's purpose.

items.text:
Notice: Updated Policy for the Campus Shuttle Service

Date: November 3
Departure Point: Carver Student Center, North Entrance

Beginning November 10, the campus shuttle will operate on a revised schedule to better serve evening classes. Shuttles will run every 20 minutes from 7:00 AM to 11:00 PM on weekdays and every 40 minutes on weekends. The late-night route to the residence halls has been extended to midnight.

All riders are reminded of the following policies:

- A valid student ID must be shown to the driver upon boarding.
- A semester shuttle pass may be purchased for $45 at the transit office in Carver Student Center.
- Single rides are free for students but cost $2.00 for guests and visitors.
- Bicycles are permitted only on the exterior rack and must be secured by the rider.

The transit office encourages students to download the TrackMyRide app, through which real-time shuttle locations can be viewed. Lost items are held at the transit office for fourteen days before being donated.

Riders with accessibility needs should request a lift-equipped shuttle at least one day in advance by calling the transit office at 555-0148.

options.B: Every 20 minutes between morning and night
options.C: Every 14 minutes during peak class times
options.D: Every hour after the late-night route ends

questions.explanation: The notice states shuttles will run every 20 minutes from 7:00 AM to 11:00 PM on weekdays. Option A uses the real 40-minute interval but misapplies it (that is the weekend frequency). Option C borrows the number 14 from the lost-items policy and attaches it to the wrong context. Option D fabricates an hourly schedule not mentioned in the text.

options.B: They are not allowed to ride the campus shuttle at all
options.C: They can request a lift-equipped shuttle without notice
options.D: They will pay a fare that students are not charged

questions.explanation: Single rides are free for students but cost $2.00 for guests and visitors, implying visitors pay a fare students avoid. Option A wrongly requires the $45 semester pass, which is optional. Option B contradicts the policy by denying visitors access entirely. Option C ignores the rule that lift-equipped shuttles require one day's advance notice.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this notice?

options.A: To announce a revised shuttle schedule and rider policies
options.B: To promote the download of the TrackMyRide tracking app
options.C: To explain how lost items are stored at the transit office
options.D: To describe the cost of a semester pass for the shuttle

questions.explanation: The notice's overall purpose is to inform riders of the new shuttle schedule and the policies that govern its use. Option B captures only one supporting detail (the app). Option C is too narrow, addressing just the lost-items rule. Option D focuses on a single price rather than the full set of changes.

items.text:
Notice: Fall Sustainability Fair on the Quad

Date: October 5
Location: Main Quad, in front of Bradley Hall

The Office of Campus Life invites all students to attend the annual Sustainability Fair on Saturday, October 18, from 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM. The event will be held on the Main Quad and will move indoors to the Bradley Hall atrium in case of rain.

This year's fair will feature local vendors, student organizations, and hands-on workshops on composting and energy conservation. Attendees can look forward to the following:

- A free reusable water bottle for the first 200 visitors.
- A clothing swap where students may exchange gently used items at no cost.
- A repair station where volunteers will fix small electronics and bicycles.
- A raffle for a $50 campus bookstore gift card; tickets are $1.00 each.

Students who wish to volunteer at the fair should sign up through the Campus Life portal by October 12. Volunteers will receive a free lunch and a certificate of participation.

Proceeds from the raffle support the campus community garden. For more information, please email campuslife@bradleyu.edu.

Subject: Welcome to the Fairview Tool Library

Dear Mr. Okafor,

Thanks for signing up at the Fairview Tool Library, located inside the Maple Street Community Center next to the public garden. We're glad you've joined a network of neighbors who share equipment instead of buying it.

Your membership runs for one year and costs a suggested donation of $20. With it, you can borrow up to three items at a time, from cordless drills to lawn aerators, for a seven-day period. Renewals are free if no one else has reserved the item. Late returns add a $1 charge per day to your account, so please check the due date printed on your receipt.

We also host a free repair workshop on the second Saturday of each month, from 1:00 PM to 3:00 PM. Volunteers will help you fix small appliances and bicycles at no cost, though you'll need to supply any specialty parts yourself.

To activate your card, stop by the front desk during open hours and bring a photo ID. If you have questions about reservations, just reply to this message.

Best regards,
Dana Whitfield
Fairview Tool Library

items.text:
Subject: Action Required: Parking Permit Renewal for Fall Term

Dear Ms. Castellano,

Your student parking permit for Lot C expires on August 31, and renewals for the fall term are now open through the campus transportation portal. We're sending this reminder early so you won't lose your assigned space.

The annual permit fee is $90, payable online by debit, credit, or charge to your student account. Once you've paid, your existing decal stays valid—there's no need to pick up a new sticker. Please confirm that your vehicle's license plate is current in the portal, since enforcement now relies on plate scanning rather than visual decal checks.

If you do not renew before September 5, your space will be released to a student on the waiting list, and any vehicle parked in Lot C without an active permit may be ticketed at $40 per violation. Students who no longer need a permit should cancel online to avoid being charged automatically.

Questions about billing or lot assignments can be directed to our office at parking@university.edu.

Thanks,
The Campus Transportation Office

options.D: Cancel her permit through the online portal

questions.explanation: The email says to 'confirm that your vehicle's license plate is current in the portal' because enforcement uses plate scanning. Option A reverses the stated fact ('no need to pick up a new sticker'); C and D borrow real terms but apply them to the wrong situation.

options.D: Spaces are assigned through a daily online lottery

questions.explanation: Because 'enforcement now relies on plate scanning rather than visual decal checks,' identification is plate-based. Option B reverses the new system back to decals; A and D introduce procedures not mentioned.

questions.explanation: The email's purpose is to prompt timely renewal of the permit before the deadline. Option A contradicts the text; C and D capture topically related but incorrect purposes the email never serves.

Notice: Temporary Closure of the Hartwell Library Reading Room

The third-floor Reading Room in Hartwell Library will be closed for floor refinishing from Monday, June 9, through Friday, June 13. Crews will be on-site each day from 7:00 AM to 6:00 PM, and the area must remain sealed while the new surface cures.

During the closure, please note the following arrangements:

• Quiet study seating will be relocated to Room 210 on the second floor, open 8:00 AM to 10:00 PM.
• Reserved materials normally shelved in the Reading Room can be picked up at the main circulation desk on the first floor.
• Group study rooms remain available and may be booked online up to 48 hours in advance.

Students with belongings stored in the Reading Room lockers must remove them by 5:00 PM on Friday, June 6. Items left after that time will be held at the lost-and-found desk for two weeks and then donated. A $5 fee applies to replace any lost locker key before the deadline.

We apologize for the inconvenience and appreciate your patience. For updates, please check the library portal or contact the help desk on the first floor.

Notice: Riverside Campus Autumn Lantern Festival

Date: Saturday, October 18
Time: 5:00 PM to 9:00 PM
Location: North Quad (Rain location: Bauer Field House)

The Office of Student Life invites all students, staff, and their families to the annual Autumn Lantern Festival, a celebration of the season featuring food stalls, live folk music, and a guided evening walk along the illuminated river path.

Event highlights:

• A lantern-making station where you can decorate your own paper lantern using materials provided free of charge.
• Food and craft vendors offering seasonal snacks; most items are priced between $2 and $6, cash or campus card accepted.
• A short outdoor concert beginning at 7:00 PM near the central fountain.

Please keep the following in mind. The river path closes promptly at 8:45 PM for safety, so begin the walk early. Pets are not permitted on the path, though they are welcome elsewhere in the quad on a leash. Students who wish to volunteer at the lantern station should sign up through the Student Life portal by October 13; volunteers receive a free festival mug and priority parking in Lot B.

For questions, please contact the Office of Student Life at the Reynolds Center.

items.text:
Notice: Campus Wi-Fi Security Certificate Update

The Office of Information Technology will update the security certificate for the campus wireless network, CampusNet, on Monday, March 10, from 6:00 AM to 9:00 AM. During this window, all devices will be temporarily disconnected and must be reconnected manually once the update is complete.

To restore access after the update, students and staff are asked to:

- Forget the old CampusNet network in your device settings
- Reconnect using your university portal username and password
- Accept the new certificate when prompted on screen

Devices that are not updated will lose network access until the new certificate is installed. The IT Help Desk in Carver Hall, Room 118, will offer free walk-in support from 9:00 AM to 4:00 PM throughout the week. Personal devices brought in for manual configuration after hours are subject to a $15 service fee.

Users are reminded never to share portal credentials, as the IT staff will never request your password by email. Questions may be directed to helpdesk@university.edu.

options.A: Remove the old CampusNet network from device settings
options.B: Visit the Help Desk in Carver Hall, Room 118
options.C: Email portal credentials to the IT support staff
options.D: Pay the fifteen-dollar manual configuration charge

questions.explanation: The bulleted steps begin with 'Forget the old CampusNet network in your device settings,' which option A paraphrases as removing the old network. The other options reference real passage details (the Help Desk location, credentials, the fee) but none is the first listed step.

options.A: It will receive the new certificate automatically overnight
options.B: It will be unable to connect until the certificate is installed
options.C: It will be charged a fifteen-dollar fee for reconnection
options.D: It will keep working normally on the old CampusNet network

questions.explanation: The notice states devices that are not updated 'will lose network access until the new certificate is installed,' implying continued disconnection. Option D reverses this logic; option A invents automatic installation; option C misapplies the after-hours fee, which is for personal devices brought in for manual setup.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this notice?

options.A: To advertise free walk-in support hours at the IT Help Desk
options.B: To explain how to reconnect after a Wi-Fi certificate update
options.C: To warn that the campus wireless network is being discontinued
options.D: To announce a new fifteen-dollar charge for all device repairs

questions.explanation: The notice's overall aim is to inform users about the certificate update and guide them through reconnecting. Option A captures only one detail; option C contradicts the text (the network continues); option D exaggerates the limited after-hours fee into a universal repair charge.

Subject: Flu Shot Appointment Confirmation

Dear Ms. Tanaka,

Thank you for scheduling your seasonal flu shot at the Student Health Center. This message confirms your appointment for Thursday, October 17, at 2:30 PM in the Immunization Suite, Wellman Hall, Room 105.

Please arrive ten minutes early and bring your student ID card and your insurance information. The flu shot itself is free for all enrolled students, but if you miss your appointment without canceling at least 24 hours in advance, a $20 no-show fee will be added to your account. You can cancel or reschedule through the online health portal at any time.

We recommend wearing a short-sleeved shirt for easy access, and you're welcome to stay in our waiting area for fifteen minutes afterward if you'd like to be monitored. Most students experience no side effects beyond mild soreness.

If you have any questions, feel free to reply to this email or call us at (555) 204-8810.

Best regards,
Nurse Daniel Ortiz
Student Health Center

options.C: Her completed online health portal registration form
options.D: A note confirming she canceled her previous booking

options.D: To confirm appointment details and preparation instructions

questions.explanation: The email's central function is to confirm the booking and tell Ms. Tanaka how to prepare. Options A and B each capture only a single detail, and option C overstates the message as general promotion rather than a personal confirmation.

items.text:
Notice: Library Extended Hours and Locker Policy for Finals Week

Beginning Monday, December 9, the Hartwell Library will extend its operating hours for finals week. The main reading room and second-floor study commons will remain open from 7:00 AM until 2:00 AM daily through Friday, December 20.

To support quiet study, the following guidelines are now in effect:

- Group discussion is permitted only in the basement collaboration rooms
- Food is not allowed in the reading room; covered drinks are acceptable
- Overnight study lockers may be reserved at the circulation desk

Lockers are available on a first-come basis and require a refundable $10 deposit, which is returned when the key is brought back by December 20. Lockers not emptied by closing on the final day will be cleared, and their contents will be held at the front desk for one week.

Students are encouraged to reserve a locker early, as demand is high during this period. The library will be closed on December 21 and 22 for cleaning. Please direct any questions to the circulation desk or email library@university.edu.

questions.explanation: The notice says contents of un-emptied lockers 'will be held at the front desk for one week,' implying temporary storage. Option A contradicts this by claiming immediate disposal; options C and D invent outcomes not mentioned.

items.text:
Subject: Confirmation — Volunteer Orientation for Spring Campus Cleanup

Dear Mr. Okafor,

Thank you for signing up to volunteer for the Spring Campus Cleanup. We're writing to confirm your spot and to share a few important details before the event.

Your orientation session is scheduled for Wednesday, April 2, at 5:00 PM in the Greenwood Student Union, Room 220. The orientation lasts about forty-five minutes and covers safety procedures, team assignments, and the use of equipment. Attendance is required for all first-time volunteers.

The cleanup itself takes place on Saturday, April 5, from 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM. Gloves, bags, and tools will be provided at no cost, but volunteers should wear closed-toe shoes and bring a reusable water bottle. A free lunch will be served afterward to everyone who completes a shift.

If you cannot attend the orientation, please let us know by April 1 so we can offer your place to someone on the waiting list. You can reach us anytime at greencrew@university.edu.

Thanks again for helping keep our campus beautiful!

Warm regards,
Priya Menon
Volunteer Coordinator

format_metadata.subject: Confirmation — Volunteer Orientation for Spring Campus Cleanup

options.A: Their own gloves, bags, and cleanup tools
options.B: Closed-toe shoes and a reusable water bottle
options.C: A signed copy of the orientation safety form
options.D: A packed lunch to eat after their shift ends

questions.explanation: The email states volunteers 'should wear closed-toe shoes and bring a reusable water bottle.' Option A reverses a fact, since gloves, bags, and tools are provided; option C invents a form; option D contradicts the free lunch offered afterward.
